questions.question: Explain the concept of Process Identifier (PID) and its importance in operating systems.
keyPoints: PID is a unique numeric identifier assigned by the OS kernel to each process
It enables the OS to track, manage, and coordinate multiple concurrent processes
Essential for process scheduling, inter-process communication, and resource allocation
Allows parent-child relationship tracking through getpid() and getppid()

questions.question: Differentiate between zombie and orphan processes with examples.
keyPoints: Zombie: Terminated process whose parent hasn't read exit status using wait() - still in process table
Orphan: Running child process whose parent has terminated - adopted by init (PID 1)
Zombies consume process table entry but no resources
Orphans continue execution under init's supervision

questions.question: Describe the role of init process (PID 1) in Unix/Linux systems.
keyPoints: Init is the first user-space process started after kernel boot
It has PID 1 and is the ancestor of all other user processes
It adopts orphan processes and performs wait() to prevent zombies
It is the parent of all detached daemons and orphaned processes
Responsible for system initialization and runlevel management

questions.question: Explain the fork() system call with respect to PID handling.
keyPoints: fork() creates a new process (child) that is a copy of parent
Parent receives child's PID as return value
Child receives 0 as return value to identify itself
Child gets a unique PID assigned by the kernel
fork() failure returns -1

questions.question: What is the Process Table? Explain its significance with respect to PIDs.
keyPoints: Process Table is a kernel data structure storing information about all active processes
Each entry contains PID, process state, program counter, memory info, scheduling parameters
Allows OS to uniquely identify each process using PID
Used for process management, scheduling, and resource allocation
PID serves as the primary key/index into the process table

questions.question: Explain getpid() and getppid() system calls with their return values and usage.
keyPoints: getpid() returns the process ID of the calling process
getppid() returns the parent process ID of the calling process
Both take no arguments and return pid_t type
Used for process identification and tracking parent-child relationships
Essential for process monitoring and debugging

questions.question: Discuss the process hierarchy in Unix/Linux systems. Explain how PIDs and PPIDs are used to establish parent-child relationships, and the role of special PIDs (0 and 1).
keyPoints: All processes (except init) have a parent - forms tree hierarchy
PID 0: Swapper/Scheduler - kernel process for memory management
PID 1: Init process - first user process, ancestor of all others
Each process has unique PID and knows its parent via getppid()
When parent dies, child becomes orphan and is adopted by init
Process table maintains this hierarchy for process management
wait() system call allows parent to retrieve child status
This hierarchy enables process monitoring, cleanup, and resource inheritance

questions.question: Explain the life cycle of a process with respect to fork(), exec(), wait(), and exit() system calls. How do these calls relate to PID management?
keyPoints: fork() creates new process - parent gets child's PID, child gets 0
exec() replaces process image but retains same PID
Parent can use wait() to suspend until child terminates and get its PID
exit() terminates process and makes it eligible for zombie state
If parent calls wait(), zombie is cleaned up, PID released
If parent dies before wait(), child becomes orphan adopted by init
PID remains constant throughout process lifetime except at creation
Process table entry released only after wait() is called on terminated process

questions.question: What are Process Groups and Sessions? Explain how they relate to PIDs.
keyPoints: Process Group: Collection of related processes identified by PGID
Group leader's PID becomes the PGID for all group members
Used for job control - signals can be sent to all group members
Session: Collection of process groups identified by SID
Session leader's PID becomes the SID
Sessions enable terminal control and login session management
Each session can have a controlling terminal
Process hierarchy: Session contains process groups, groups contain processes, each has unique PID
